derived_doctrine: Define state order, units, graph resolution, action timing and reset maps before selecting a method.
Establish an uncontrolled model and an independent numerical baseline before complex control or learning.
Record graph source, resolved parameters, solver configuration, convergence and invariant errors for every reported run.
Accept documented graph formats through a provenance-aware adapter and preserve full-graph statistics separately from reduced dense examples.
Record heterogeneous parameter arrays and compare them with matched-mean homogeneous profiles.
Use one layered progression from dynamics to control, games, impulses and network implementation.
Define pre-action observation, action selection, optional reset, zero-order-hold flow, integration and interval reward in that order.
Separate running costs from one-time impulse costs and distinguish sampled actions from continuous-time controls.
Match a learner to the action/information structure and retain no-action, fixed, rule-based and budget-matched random baselines.
Evaluate deterministic policies across multiple training seeds and held-out graph/parameter settings using physical metrics as well as reward.
Treat response matrices and unilateral deviations as empirical diagnostics rather than equilibrium proofs.
Replace synthetic graph construction through an external-topology adapter rather than changing policy logic.
Begin by declaring the unknown object; derive network outputs and loss terms from that declaration.
Separate observed data points from collocation points and log every loss component rather than only the weighted total.
Use an identifiable heterogeneous parameterization and report gauge or correlation ambiguities.
Hold out nodes and times from every training loss, compare homogeneous misspecification and retain failed fits.
Keep synthetic generated truth for recovery tests, but add a separate real-data adapter and calibration vocabulary for field observations.
Emit explicit node/time observation and holdout records so evaluation masks are auditable.
